name: Art & Design
description: Explore the world of famous artists, movements, and masterpieces.

questions.text: Who painted the Mona Lisa?
questions.explanation: Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa between approximately 1503 and 1519.

questions.text: In which museum is the Mona Lisa currently displayed?
questions.explanation: The Mona Lisa has been on permanent display at the Louvre Museum in Paris since 1797.

questions.text: Which artist is known for painting 'The Starry Night'?
questions.explanation: Vincent van Gogh painted The Starry Night in June 1889 while at the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum.

questions.text: What art movement is characterized by small brushstrokes and capturing light in outdoor scenes?
questions.explanation: Impressionism, pioneered by Monet and others, focused on capturing fleeting light effects with loose brushwork.

questions.text: Who sculpted 'David', the famous Renaissance marble statue?
questions.explanation: Michelangelo sculpted David between 1501 and 1504; it stands in the Galleria dell'Accademia in Florence.

questions.text: What does 'chiaroscuro' refer to in painting?
options: The use of bright primary colors
The contrast between light and shadow
A painting technique using only gold leaf
questions.explanation: Chiaroscuro is an Italian technique using strong contrasts between light and dark to create volume and depth.

questions.text: Which Spanish artist co-founded the Cubist movement?
questions.explanation: Pablo Picasso and Georges Braque co-founded Cubism around 1907-1908, revolutionizing modern art.

questions.text: Where is the Sistine Chapel ceiling, painted by Michelangelo, located?
questions.explanation: The Sistine Chapel is located in Vatican City, Rome, and Michelangelo painted its ceiling from 1508 to 1512.

questions.text: Which artist is famous for melting clocks in his surrealist paintings?
questions.explanation: Salvador Dalí's The Persistence of Memory (1931) features iconic melting watches representing fluid time.

questions.text: What is a 'fresco'?
options: A painting on canvas with oil
A painting done on wet plaster
questions.explanation: A fresco is a mural painted on freshly laid, still-wet plaster, allowing pigment to bond with the wall.

questions.text: Which painting features a woman screaming on a bridge under a swirling sky?
questions.explanation: The Scream (1893) by Edvard Munch depicts an anguished figure against a turbulent orange sky.

questions.text: Which art movement was Marcel Duchamp associated with?
questions.explanation: Marcel Duchamp was a key figure in the Dada movement, famous for his 'readymades' like Fountain (1917).

questions.text: What technique involves applying thick paint to create texture on canvas?
questions.explanation: Impasto is a technique where paint is applied very thickly, retaining brush or palette knife marks.

questions.text: Who painted 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' (c. 1665)?
questions.explanation: Johannes (Jan) Vermeer painted Girl with a Pearl Earring; it hangs in the Mauritshuis in The Hague.

questions.text: Which American artist is known for Campbell's Soup Cans and Marilyn Monroe prints?
questions.explanation: Andy Warhol was the leading figure of Pop Art, using commercial imagery to critique consumer culture.

questions.text: In which century did the Renaissance art movement originate?
questions.explanation: The Renaissance began in the 14th century (Trecento) in Italian city-states like Florence and Venice.

questions.text: What is the world's largest art museum?
questions.explanation: The Louvre in Paris is the world's largest art museum by exhibition space, covering over 72,000 square meters.

questions.text: Which movement rejected traditional forms and celebrated industrial age aesthetics in the early 20th century?
questions.explanation: Italian Futurism (from 1909) glorified speed, technology, and dynamic movement of the modern machine age.

questions.text: What painting technique uses tiny dots of color to create an image?
questions.explanation: Pointillism, developed by Georges Seurat, uses small distinct dots of color that blend optically from a distance.

questions.text: Who created 'The Birth of Venus' (c. 1484–1486)?
questions.explanation: Sandro Botticelli painted The Birth of Venus for the Medici family; it hangs in the Uffizi Gallery, Florence.

questions.text: Which sculptor created 'The Thinker'?
questions.explanation: Auguste Rodin created The Thinker in 1880 as part of The Gates of Hell; many casts exist worldwide.

questions.text: What is the term for a painting or artwork on two panels?
questions.explanation: A diptych is an artwork consisting of two panels, often hinged together, common in medieval and Renaissance art.

questions.text: Which Mexican artist is known for self-portraits featuring a unibrow?
questions.explanation: Frida Kahlo created around 55 self-portraits, often depicting her pain and Mexican cultural identity.

questions.text: What does 'sfumato' mean in art?
options: A mosaic style using colored glass
A smoky blending technique with no sharp outlines
Painting with fingers instead of brushes
A style of abstract geometric art
questions.explanation: Sfumato (Italian for 'smoke') is Leonardo da Vinci's technique of blending tones without visible transitions.

questions.text: Which Baroque painter was known for dramatic light and shadow and was also a convicted killer?
questions.explanation: Caravaggio killed Ranuccio Tomassoni in 1606 and fled Rome; his dramatic tenebrism revolutionized Baroque painting.

questions.text: What is the name for the art of arranging flowers, originating in Japan?
questions.explanation: Ikebana is the Japanese art of flower arrangement, emphasizing minimalism and harmony with nature.

questions.text: In what year did Pablo Picasso paint 'Guernica'?
questions.explanation: Picasso painted Guernica in 1937 in response to the Nazi bombing of the Basque town of Guernica during the Spanish Civil War.

questions.text: Which art movement was characterized by distorted imagery expressing emotional experience?
questions.explanation: Expressionism distorted reality for emotional effect, prominent in Germany in the early 20th century.

questions.text: Who designed the Guggenheim Museum in Bilbao, Spain?
questions.explanation: Frank Gehry designed the titanium-clad Guggenheim Bilbao, which opened in 1997 and sparked the 'Bilbao Effect'.

questions.text: What is the primary medium used in a lithograph?
options: Stone or metal plate with ink and grease
questions.explanation: Lithography uses the chemical repulsion of oil and water on a stone or metal plate to transfer images.

questions.text: Which Dutch master is famous for works like 'The Night Watch' (1642)?
questions.explanation: Rembrandt van Rijn painted The Night Watch, the largest and most famous work in his collection.

questions.text: What term describes art created from everyday, non-art materials?
questions.explanation: Assemblage is a technique using found or everyday objects combined to create three-dimensional art.

questions.text: Which city is home to the Uffizi Gallery?
questions.explanation: The Uffizi Gallery in Florence, Italy, houses one of the finest collections of Renaissance art in the world.

questions.text: What color is produced by mixing red and blue pigments?
questions.explanation: Mixing red and blue pigments produces purple (or violet), a secondary color in traditional color theory.

questions.text: Which movement, founded by André Breton in 1924, explored dreams and the unconscious?
questions.explanation: André Breton founded Surrealism in 1924, heavily influenced by Freud's theories of the unconscious mind.

questions.text: What type of paint uses water-soluble pigments with quick drying time?
questions.explanation: Acrylic paint is water-soluble but dries quickly to a water-resistant finish, making it very versatile.

questions.text: Who created the mobile sculptures that hang and move with air currents?
questions.explanation: Alexander Calder invented the mobile—a kinetic sculpture that moves with air currents—in the 1930s.

questions.text: What is 'trompe-l'oeil' in art?
options: A technique of painting on silk
Painting that creates a realistic illusion of three-dimensional space
questions.explanation: Trompe-l'oeil (French: 'deceives the eye') uses realistic imagery to create optical illusions of three-dimensional space.

questions.text: Which Impressionist painter was known for painting ballet dancers?
questions.explanation: Edgar Degas made over 1,500 works depicting ballet dancers, his most celebrated and recognizable subject.

questions.text: What art movement emerged in New York in the 1940s-50s, emphasizing spontaneous, large-scale painting?
questions.explanation: Abstract Expressionism, featuring artists like Pollock and de Kooning, was the first major American art movement.

questions.text: Which artist dripped and poured paint onto canvases laid on the floor?
questions.explanation: Jackson Pollock developed 'drip painting', a technique of pouring and dripping paint onto horizontal canvases.

questions.text: What is the name of the Japanese art of paper folding?
questions.explanation: Origami is the traditional Japanese art of folding paper into decorative shapes and figures.

questions.text: Which British artist is known for works involving dead animals preserved in formaldehyde?
questions.explanation: Damien Hirst's Natural History series features animals like sharks and cows preserved in formaldehyde vitrines.

questions.text: What is the golden ratio approximately equal to?
questions.explanation: The golden ratio (phi) ≈ 1.618, and has been used by artists and architects for centuries for harmonious proportions.

questions.text: In which country did the Baroque art movement originate?
questions.explanation: Baroque art originated in Rome, Italy, around 1600 as part of the Catholic Counter-Reformation.

questions.text: Which artist painted 'Water Lilies', a series of approximately 250 works?
questions.explanation: Claude Monet painted around 250 Water Lilies works, inspired by his garden at Giverny, France.

questions.text: What does the art term 'en plein air' mean?
options: Painting at night using artificial light
Painting with a palette knife only
questions.explanation: En plein air (French: 'in the open air') refers to the practice of painting outdoors to capture natural light.

questions.text: Which ancient wonder involved a colossal statue at the entrance to a harbor?
questions.explanation: The Colossus of Rhodes was a giant statue of the sun god Helios at the entrance to Rhodes harbor, built c. 280 BC.

questions.text: What is the primary pigment that gives ultramarine blue its color?
questions.explanation: Historically, ultramarine blue was made from ground lapis lazuli gemstone, making it more expensive than gold.

questions.text: Which street artist is known for politically charged, stenciled works appearing in public spaces?
questions.explanation: Banksy is the anonymous British street artist whose satirical stencil artworks appear globally in public spaces.
